subject: science
title: Cells — the building blocks
subtitle: What every living thing is made of

sections.heading: What is a cell?

predict.question: Before you read: what's the smallest part of a living thing that's still alive on its own?
predict.answer: A cell. It's the smallest unit that can take in food, grow, and copy itself. Anything smaller (like an organelle or a molecule) only works AS PART OF a cell.

sections.body:
Every living thing is made of cells. You are made of trillions. A cell is the smallest unit that can do all the jobs of being alive — taking in food, getting rid of waste, growing, and copying itself.

There are two big families: animal cells and plant cells. They share a lot but plant cells have extra parts because plants make their own food.

quickfact.label: typical animal cell width — ~50 fit across a dot

sections.heading: The main parts (organelles)
list: Nucleus — the control centre. Holds the DNA instructions.
Cytoplasm — the jelly inside, where chemical reactions happen.
Cell membrane — the skin that decides what gets in and out.
Mitochondria — release energy from food (in animal AND plant cells).
Cell wall — only in plant cells. Stiff outer layer for shape and support.
Chloroplasts — only in plant cells. Green dots that catch sunlight to make food (photosynthesis).
Vacuole — only in plant cells (large one). A bag of water that keeps the plant rigid.

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: Animal cells = NCM (Nucleus, Cytoplasm, Membrane). Plant cells add WCV (Wall, Chloroplasts, Vacuole).

example.title: What only plants have (and why)
steps: Cell wall — gives the plant its rigid shape (animals have a skeleton instead).
Chloroplasts — catch sunlight to make food (animals eat food, no need).
Big vacuole — stores water and keeps the plant firm (animals are mostly soft tissue).

why_prompt.question: Why don't animal cells need chloroplasts?
why_prompt.answer: Animals eat food that already has energy locked in it (plants, or other animals that ate plants). Plants can't move to find food, so they make their own from sunlight — that's what chloroplasts do.

sections.heading: How small are they?
sections.body: Most cells are too small to see without a microscope. A typical animal cell is about 0.02 mm wide — you'd fit fifty of them across the dot above this 'i'.

teach_back.prompt: Explain animal vs plant cells to a 7-year-old in 2 sentences.
checklist: Said which one has a cell wall
teach_back.model_answer: Plant cells have a stiff outer wall and little green bits called chloroplasts that grab sunlight to make food. Animal cells are softer and can't make their own food, so animals have to eat.

tips: Easy memory: animal cells = NCM (Nucleus, Cytoplasm, Membrane). Plant cells add WCV (Wall, Chloroplasts, Vacuole).
Plants are green because chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, which reflects green light.
Mitochondria are sometimes called the cell's 'power plants' — they release energy from glucose.

subject: science
title: Particles & states of matter
subtitle: Why solids, liquids and gases behave differently

sections.heading: Everything is made of particles
sections.body: Every substance — your desk, your drink, the air — is made of tiny particles you can't see. The way those particles are arranged decides whether the substance is a solid, a liquid or a gas.

sections.heading: The three states
list: Solid — particles packed tight in a regular pattern. They vibrate but can't move past each other. Fixed shape, fixed volume.
Liquid — particles still touching but can slide past each other. Takes the shape of its container, fixed volume.
Gas — particles spread far apart, moving fast in all directions. Fills any container, expands to fit.

callout.title: Water is weird
callout.body: Most substances are denser as solids than as liquids. Water is the opposite — ice is LESS dense than liquid water, which is why ice floats. This matters for life: in winter, ice floats on top of lakes and insulates the water below, keeping fish alive.

sections.heading: Heating and cooling
sections.body:
Add heat → particles get more energy → they move more. Solid melts to liquid. Liquid evaporates (or boils) to gas. Cooling reverses each step: gas condenses to liquid, liquid freezes to solid.

The particles themselves don't change. Only their arrangement and how much they move.

example.title: What happens when ice melts
example.intro: Step through what's happening to the water particles:
steps: Ice particles are locked in a regular pattern, vibrating in place.
Heat is added → particles vibrate harder.
At 0°C, particles have enough energy to break free of their fixed positions — the ice melts.
Particles can now slide past each other → liquid water.
example.outro: Add more heat and you'll reach 100°C — particles break free of each other entirely → steam (gas).

quickfact.label: boiling point of water at sea level

teach_back.prompt: Explain why ice floats on water to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Mentioned ice is less dense than water
teach_back.model_answer: When water freezes into ice, the particles lock into a pattern that's actually more spaced out than liquid water. Same particles, bigger space — that makes ice lighter (less dense), so it floats on top.

tips: Water is an unusual exception: ice (solid) is LESS dense than water (liquid). That's why ice floats.
Gases are mostly empty space — that's why they squish so easily under pressure.
When you smell something, gas particles from that thing have travelled through the air to your nose.

subject: science
title: The Universe — Sun, Earth, Moon
subtitle: How our corner of space works

sections.heading: The Solar System
sections.body: Our Sun is a star. Around it orbit eight planets, lots of moons, asteroids and comets. The order of the planets, closest to the Sun first: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.

quickfact.label: average distance from the Earth to the Sun (1 AU)

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: "My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Noodles" — Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.

sections.heading: Why we have day and night
sections.body: Earth spins on its own axis once every 24 hours. The side facing the Sun has day. The side facing away has night. The Sun isn't moving across the sky — WE are moving.

callout.title: Common misconception
callout.body: The Sun does NOT 'rise' and 'set'. The horizon rotates away from the Sun (sunset) or towards it (sunrise) as Earth spins.

sections.heading: Why we have seasons
sections.body: Earth's axis is tilted at about 23.5°. As Earth orbits the Sun once a year, different hemispheres point towards the Sun at different times. When it's tilted towards us → longer days, more direct sunlight → summer. Tilted away → winter.

example.title: June in the UK vs December in the UK
steps: June: northern hemisphere tilts towards the Sun.
Sun is high in the sky, daylight is long (~16+ hours).
Sunlight hits the ground steeply → concentrated heat → summer.
December: same Earth, opposite point in orbit. North tilts away.
Sun is low, days are short (~8 hours), light hits at a shallow angle → spread thin → winter.
example.outro: Note: it's NOT because Earth is closer or further from the Sun — that barely changes.

sections.heading: Phases of the Moon
sections.body: The Sun always lights up half the Moon. As the Moon orbits Earth (about every 27 days), we see different proportions of the lit half. New Moon (lit half facing away) → crescent → half → full Moon (lit half facing us) → and back again.

quickfact.label: time for the Moon to orbit Earth once

teach_back.prompt: Explain why we have day and night to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Mentioned the Sun stays still (from our view)
Didn't say 'the Sun goes round the Earth'
teach_back.model_answer: The Earth spins like a slow top — once every 24 hours. When your side faces the Sun it's day; when your side faces away it's night. The Sun isn't moving across the sky — we are.

tips: Mnemonic for planet order: "My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Noodles".
Pluto isn't on the list — it was reclassified as a 'dwarf planet' in 2006.
Seasons are about TILT, not distance. Earth is actually closest to the Sun in January.

subject: maths
title: Triangles, polygons & shape names
subtitle: Naming shapes by their sides and angles

sections.heading: Triangles by their sides
list: Equilateral — all 3 sides equal, all 3 angles equal (each 60°).
Isosceles — exactly 2 sides equal. The two angles opposite those equal sides are also equal.
Scalene — all 3 sides different, all 3 angles different.
Right-angled — one angle is exactly 90° (a right angle).

callout.title: Quick spot
callout.body: Equal sides ↔ equal opposite angles. Always. So if a triangle has two equal sides, the angles opposite them must match.

sections.heading: The angle rule
sections.body: The three angles inside ANY triangle add up to 180°. So if you know two angles, you can always work out the third by subtracting them from 180°.

example.title: Find the missing angle
example.intro: A triangle has angles of 70° and 55°. Find the third:
steps: Add the known angles: 70 + 55 = 125°.
Subtract from 180°: 180 − 125 = 55°.
The missing angle is 55°.
example.outro: Notice: two angles are 55° → this is also an isosceles triangle.

quickfact.label: angles in any triangle add to this

sections.heading: Polygons by side count
list: 3 sides — triangle
4 sides — quadrilateral (square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite are all quadrilaterals)
5 sides — pentagon
6 sides — hexagon
7 sides — heptagon
8 sides — octagon
10 sides — decagon

callout.title: Greek prefix tip
callout.body: Penta = 5, hexa = 6, octa = 8 (think octopus, eight legs), deca = 10 (decade = 10 years).

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a 7-year-old how to tell a hexagon from an octagon.
teach_back.model_answer: Count the sides. A hexagon has 6 sides (think honeycomb). An octagon has 8 sides (think OCTopus — 8 legs).

tips: A 'regular' polygon means all sides equal AND all angles equal. A regular triangle = equilateral. A regular quadrilateral = a square.
Quick check: angles in a triangle ALWAYS = 180°. Angles in a quadrilateral always = 360°.
Sum of interior angles in any polygon = (n − 2) × 180°, where n = number of sides.

subject: maths
title: Square & cube numbers
subtitle: Multiplying a number by itself

sections.heading: Square numbers
sections.body:
A square number is what you get when you multiply a whole number by itself.

1² = 1 × 1 = 1
2² = 2 × 2 = 4
3² = 3 × 3 = 9
4² = 4 × 4 = 16
5² = 5 × 5 = 25

The little 2 means "squared" — you literally make a square that's that wide and that tall.

callout.title: Why "squared"?
callout.body: Because 5² is the area of a real 5-by-5 square: 25 little unit squares. The diagram above shows 1², 2², 3², 4² as actual squares.

sections.heading: Cube numbers
sections.body:
A cube number is what you get when you multiply a whole number by itself THREE times. The little 3 means "cubed".

1³ = 1 × 1 × 1 = 1
2³ = 2 × 2 × 2 = 8
3³ = 3 × 3 × 3 = 27
4³ = 4 × 4 × 4 = 64
5³ = 5 × 5 × 5 = 125

sections.heading: Square root
sections.body: The square root is the reverse of squaring. The square root of 49 is 7, because 7 × 7 = 49. The symbol is √. So √49 = 7.

example.title: Work out √144
steps: Ask: what number times itself equals 144?
Try 10: 10 × 10 = 100 (too small).
example.outro: Memorising 1² to 12² makes square roots up to 144 instant.

teach_back.prompt: Explain what 5² means to a 7-year-old in 2 sentences.
teach_back.model_answer: 5² means 5 times 5. Imagine making a square that's 5 wide and 5 tall — you'd need 25 little tiles to fill it, and 25 is the answer.

tips: Memorise the squares from 1² to 12² — they come up in tons of questions.
If you square a negative number, you get a POSITIVE answer (negative × negative = positive).

subject: english
title: Word classes — the parts of speech
subtitle: What each word in a sentence is doing

sections.heading: The main word classes
list: Noun — a person, place, thing or idea. (cat, London, freedom)
Verb — a doing or being word. (run, is, think)
Adjective — describes a noun. (happy, blue, ancient)
Adverb — describes a verb (or an adjective). Often ends in -ly. (quickly, very, well)
Pronoun — stands in for a noun. (he, she, it, they, we)
Preposition — shows position or direction. (under, above, between, into)
Conjunction — joins words or sentences. (and, but, because, although)
Determiner — comes before a noun, points it out. (the, a, this, my, some)

callout.title: The cleanest split
callout.body: Adjectives describe NOUNS. Adverbs describe VERBS (or other adjectives). Mixing these up is the #1 wrong-class error.

sections.heading: How to spot them in a sentence

example.title: Break down: "The huge dog barked loudly under the table."
steps: The → determiner (points out which dog)
huge → adjective (describes 'dog')
dog → noun (the thing)
barked → verb (the action)
loudly → adverb (describes how it barked)
under → preposition (position)
the → determiner
table → noun

sections.heading: Quick tests you can run
sections.body:
If you can put 'a' or 'the' in front of a word and it makes sense, it's almost certainly a noun (a cat, the freedom).

If the word ends in -ly and adds info to a verb, it's an adverb (quickly, slowly, happily).

If the word can be replaced with 'is/was/runs/jumps' and the sentence still makes sense, that slot wants a verb.

callout.title: Watch out
callout.body: Some words swap class depending on use. "Run" is a verb in "I run" but a noun in "a long run". Always check what it's DOING in the sentence.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.body: Examiners ask you to identify word classes because they want to see you can analyse HOW a sentence works. Once you can spot a verb, an adjective, a noun cleanly, you can talk about the writer's choices: "the verb 'crept' makes the cat seem nervous".

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a 7-year-old what a verb is.
teach_back.model_answer: A verb is a doing word — it tells you what someone is DOING in a sentence. 'Run', 'jump', 'eat', 'sleep', 'is' — they're all verbs.

tips: Adverbs often end in -ly — but not always (well, fast, often, very).
If you can swap a word with another known one of the same class without breaking the sentence, you've identified it.
Pronouns are noun replacements. Spot them by asking: who or what could this be referring to?

subject: english
title: Similes, metaphors & co.
subtitle: How writers paint with words

sections.heading: Simile
sections.body:
A simile compares two things using the words 'like' or 'as'.

• "Her hair shone like the sun."
• "He was as quiet as a mouse."

The LIKE or AS is the giveaway.

callout.title: Why writers use it
callout.body: Similes paint a picture quickly. "He ran fast" tells. "He ran like a startled deer" shows you the panic AND the speed in one phrase.

sections.heading: Metaphor
sections.body:
A metaphor says something IS something else. It's a stronger comparison — no 'like' or 'as'.

• "The classroom was a zoo." (it WAS a zoo, not LIKE a zoo)
• "He's a rock in a crisis."

callout.title: Stronger than simile
callout.body: A metaphor commits — it claims the two things are the same. That makes the image more vivid. "Her eyes were diamonds" hits harder than "like diamonds".

sections.heading: Personification
sections.body:
Giving a non-human thing human qualities or actions.

• "The wind whispered through the trees."
• "Time crawled."

Wind doesn't actually whisper. Time doesn't actually crawl. But the writer makes them feel alive.

example.title: Spot the technique
example.intro: "The angry sea hurled itself against the cliffs."
steps: What's being described? The sea.
What human action? Being angry, hurling — both human emotions/movements.
Technique: personification — sea isn't actually angry, but the writer wants us to feel its violence.

sections.heading: Alliteration & onomatopoeia
list: Alliteration — same starting sound repeated. "Slithering, slimy snakes."
Onomatopoeia — a word that sounds like the thing it describes. "Bang!" "Crash!" "Splat!"

quickfact.label: main techniques to know — simile, metaphor, personification, alliteration, onomatopoeia

teach_back.prompt: Explain the difference between a simile and a metaphor to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Said metaphor says it IS the thing
teach_back.model_answer: A simile says one thing is LIKE another — 'her hair was like sunshine'. A metaphor says one thing IS another — 'her hair was sunshine'. The metaphor commits, which makes the picture stronger.

tips: Simile = LIKE / AS. Metaphor = IS / WAS. That's the cleanest split.
Personification almost always uses a verb a human would do (whispered, danced, laughed).
When you spot any of these, ask: 'What effect does it have? What picture does it put in my head?' That's the analysis bit examiners want.

subject: history
title: The Norman Conquest
subtitle: 1066 — the year everything changed

sections.heading: The story
sections.body:
When King Edward the Confessor died in early 1066, three different men thought they should be the next King of England. Harold Godwinson (an English earl) was crowned almost immediately. But Harald Hardrada (a Viking) and William of Normandy (a Frenchman) both invaded.

Harold beat Hardrada at Stamford Bridge in September 1066 — then had to march south because William had landed on the south coast. Harold's exhausted army met William at the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066. Harold was killed (the Bayeux Tapestry shows an arrow striking his eye). William won. He was crowned King of England on Christmas Day 1066.

quickfact.label: the year of the Battle of Hastings

callout.title: Date to nail
callout.body: 14 October 1066 — Battle of Hastings. Memorise this one date and a lot falls into place.

sections.heading: What changed afterwards
sections.body:
William didn't just win one battle — he changed England. He replaced almost every English noble with a Norman one. He built castles (mostly motte-and-bailey, made of earth and timber) all over the country to hold down the population.

He ordered the Domesday Book in 1086 — a massive survey recording who owned what, so he could tax it properly. He brought Norman French as the language of the court — that's why English now has both Saxon words like 'cow' and French-origin words like 'beef'.

example.title: Why William's victory stuck
example.intro: It wasn't just winning one battle — three things kept the Normans in power:
steps: Replace the rulers — almost every English noble was swapped for a Norman one within 5 years.
Build castles — over 500 motte-and-bailey castles thrown up across England by 1086, controlling local areas.
Count everything — the Domesday Book (1086) recorded who owned what, so William could tax it all.
example.outro: By the time William died in 1087, England's ruling class, language and landscape were Norman.

callout.title: Common confusion
callout.body: Two men called Harold/Harald in 1066. Harold Godwinson is the English king who lost at Hastings. Harald Hardrada is the Viking king who lost earlier at Stamford Bridge. Both lost. William won.

sections.heading: The feudal system
sections.body:
Society was organised as a pyramid:

• King (William) at the top — owned all the land in theory
• Barons — held big chunks from the King in return for loyalty + soldiers
• Knights — held smaller chunks from the Barons in return for fighting
• Peasants — at the bottom — worked the land

This is what the diagram above shows.

teach_back.prompt: Explain why 1066 matters to a 7-year-old in 2 sentences.
checklist: Mentioned William or the Normans winning
Didn't try to list every detail
teach_back.model_answer: In 1066 a French duke called William invaded England, won a big battle at Hastings, and made himself king. He swapped almost every English noble for a French one — that's why English now has loads of French-origin words like 'beef' and 'castle'.

tips: Date to know cold: 14 October 1066, Battle of Hastings.
Don't confuse Harold (English, 1066) with Harald (Viking, also 1066). Both lost. William won.
The Bayeux Tapestry is a 70-metre embroidery telling the Norman version of events. It's the closest thing to a contemporary news report from 1066.

subject: geography
title: Reading a map — symbols, scale, contours
subtitle: Turning a flat map into a 3D understanding

sections.heading: Map symbols
sections.body:
An OS (Ordnance Survey) map uses tiny pictures called symbols to represent things on the ground — a church, a campsite, a footpath, woodland. The map's KEY (or legend) explains what each symbol means.

Always check the key first. Symbols are standard: a blue triangle for a campsite, a black cross for a church without a tower, a brown dotted line for a footpath, etc.

callout.title: First move on any map
callout.body: Find the KEY (usually bottom-left). Two minutes spent reading it saves ten minutes of guesswork later.

sections.heading: Scale
sections.body:
Scale tells you how distance on the map matches distance in real life. Common UK map scales:

• 1 : 25,000 — 1 cm on the map = 25,000 cm = 250 m in real life. So 4 cm = 1 km.
• 1 : 50,000 — 1 cm on the map = 500 m in real life. So 2 cm = 1 km.

To find a real distance: measure on the map, then multiply by the scale.

example.title: Worked example
example.intro: On a 1 : 25,000 map, a footpath measures 6 cm. How long is it in real life?
steps: Multiply: 6 cm × 25,000 = 150,000 cm.
Convert to metres: 150,000 ÷ 100 = 1,500 m.
Convert to km: 1,500 ÷ 1,000 = 1.5 km.
Answer: the footpath is 1.5 km long.

sections.heading: Grid references
sections.body:
OS maps have a grid of squares (each 1 km × 1 km). To pinpoint a place:

• Four-figure grid reference (e.g. 2438) — gives you the bottom-left corner of one 1 km square. Read along the bottom (eastings) first, then up the side (northings).
• Six-figure grid reference (e.g. 245384) — divides each 1 km square into a 10 × 10 grid, pinpointing a 100 m square.

callout.title: Memory rule
callout.body: "Along the corridor, then up the stairs." Eastings first (the bottom-of-the-page numbers), then northings (the side-of-the-page numbers).

quickfact.label: the square pinpointed by a 6-figure grid reference

sections.heading: Contour lines
sections.body:
Brown lines on an OS map joining points of equal height. They turn a flat map into 3D in your head:

• Lines close together = steep slope (the land rises sharply over a short distance).
• Lines far apart = gentle slope.
• Concentric rings = a hill (the smallest ring at the centre is the summit). The diagram above shows this.

The number on each contour tells you the height in metres above sea level.

teach_back.prompt: Explain how to read a 4-figure grid reference to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Mentioned 'along the corridor, up the stairs'
teach_back.model_answer: Read the first two numbers ALONG (left to right). Read the next two numbers UP (bottom to top). Always 'along the corridor, then up the stairs' — that order, every time. So 23 45 means: along to 23, then up to 45.

tips: Bearings are measured CLOCKWISE from north, in degrees, written as 3 digits. So east = 090°, south = 180°, west = 270°.
Closely spaced contours on a route mean a hard climb — useful when planning a walk.
Standard 8-point compass: N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW. Each is 45° apart.

subject: computing
title: E-safety basics
subtitle: Staying safe online — the things that actually matter

sections.heading: Personal information
sections.body:
Never share your real name (full), home address, school, phone number, or family details with strangers online. This includes in chat rooms, game lobbies, and social media DMs from people you don't know in real life.

If someone you've never met asks for any of this, the right move is always: don't reply, don't send anything, and tell a trusted adult (parent, teacher).

callout.title: Big red flag
callout.body: Anyone who pressures you for personal info, photos, or to keep something secret from your parents — that's a warning sign. Tell a trusted adult straight away.

sections.heading: Strong passwords
sections.body:
A strong password:

• Is at least 8 characters long
• Mixes UPPER and lower case letters
• Includes numbers and a symbol or two
• Isn't a real word, your name, or your birthday

"Tiger123" is weak. "p@5sW0rd!92" is strong. Better still: a memorable phrase like "3Blue!Cats#JumpHigh".

example.title: Weak vs strong
steps: ❌ password — top of every hacker's first-guess list.
❌ Aaryan2014 — your name + birth year is easily guessable.
❌ qwerty — keyboard pattern, instantly tried.
✓ p@5sW0rd!92 — mixed case, numbers, symbols, no real word.
✓ 3Blue!Cats#JumpHigh — a long passphrase, easy to remember, hard to crack.

quickfact.label: minimum characters in a strong password

sections.heading: Digital footprint
sections.body:
Everything you post, like, search and login to online leaves a trail — your 'digital footprint'. Screenshots happen. Even after you 'delete' something, someone may have already saved it.

The rule of thumb: don't post anything you wouldn't be comfortable showing your grandmother, or a future employer. Embarrassing photos can resurface years later.

callout.title: The grandma test
callout.body: Before you post or send: would you be okay with your grandma seeing it? If no, don't send.

sections.heading: Copyright
sections.body:
Anything original someone creates — a song, a video, a piece of writing, a photo — is automatically theirs by law. That's copyright. Using their work without permission can be illegal, even for a school project.

If you want music for a video, look for 'royalty-free' tracks (legal to use freely) or get the creator's permission.

checklist: Decoded at least 3 of the SMART letters
teach_back.model_answer: SMART means: Stay safe (don't share your address or phone number), don't Meet anyone you only know online, don't Accept random messages or files, check info is Reliable before believing it, and Tell a trusted grown-up if anything feels weird.

tips: Set social profiles to PRIVATE by default — only people you've accepted can see your posts.
If you're being bullied online: save evidence (screenshots), don't reply, tell a trusted adult, and use the platform's REPORT button. Replying often makes it worse.
If something online makes you uncomfortable, you don't have to figure out why — just close it and tell someone. Trust the feeling.

subject: computing
title: Computational thinking
subtitle: How programmers break problems apart

sections.heading: Decomposition
sections.body:
Big problems are scary. Computational thinking starts by breaking a problem into smaller, manageable pieces.

Example: "Make a game." Decompose into: design the player character; write the movement code; design the enemies; write the score logic; design the menu screen. Now each piece is doable on its own.

callout.title: Real-world parallel
callout.body: Cooking a 3-course meal? You don't try to do it all at once. You decompose: write the menu, write the shopping list, prep starters, cook mains, plate dessert. Same idea.

sections.heading: Pattern recognition
sections.body:
Look for similarities between the smaller pieces — and similarities to problems you've already solved. If two enemies move the same way, write the movement code ONCE and use it for both. If you've made a score counter before, you can reuse the same approach.

Spotting patterns saves time.

sections.heading: Abstraction
sections.body:
Strip away the unnecessary detail. Focus only on what matters for the problem.

A London Underground map is a brilliant example of abstraction. The actual track in real life is twisty and not to scale — but the map straightens it all out and just shows you which stations connect. That's abstraction: hide what you don't need.

example.title: Abstraction in action — building a school timetable
steps: Day of the week
Time of each lesson
Subject + room number
example.outro: What you DON'T need (strip out): teacher's hair colour, classroom paint colour, weather forecast.

sections.heading: Algorithms
sections.body: Once you've decomposed, spotted patterns, and abstracted away the noise, write a step-by-step set of instructions to solve the problem. That's an algorithm. A programmer then translates the algorithm into code.

checklist: Used the phrase 'break it down' or similar
teach_back.model_answer: Decomposition means breaking a big problem into small steps. Like getting dressed: you don't think 'get dressed' as one job — you think 'put on socks, put on shirt, put on trousers'. Computers solve problems the same way.

tips: Order to remember: DPAA — Decompose, Patterns, Abstract, Algorithm.
An algorithm is the recipe. Code is the recipe written in a language a computer understands.
Whenever you find yourself doing the same thing twice in code, that's a sign you can spot a pattern and simplify.

subject: french
title: Saying your name & age
subtitle: Year 7 French — the very first phrases

sections.heading: Saying your name
sections.body:
"Je m'appelle ___" = "My name is ___" (literally "I call myself").

If someone asks you "Comment tu t'appelles?" ("What's your name?"), reply "Je m'appelle Alex" (or whatever your name is).

The "m'" is a contraction of "me" — it shrinks because the next word starts with a vowel sound. French does this all the time.

callout.title: Pronunciation
callout.body: "Je m'appelle" sounds like "zhuh ma-PELL". The final 'e' on appelle is silent.

sections.heading: Saying your age
sections.body:
Important: in French you HAVE your age. Not "I AM twelve" but "I HAVE twelve years".

"J'ai douze ans" = "I am 12 years old" (literally "I have twelve years")

If someone asks "Quel âge as-tu?" ("How old are you?"), reply "J'ai ___ ans".

callout.title: Don't say "Je suis douze"
callout.body: That literally means "I am twelve" — but it doesn't mean 12 years old in French. Always use J'AI for age.

example.title: Mini dialogue
steps: A: Comment tu t'appelles?  → "What's your name?"
B: Je m'appelle Aaryan.  → "My name is Aaryan."
A: Quel âge as-tu?  → "How old are you?"
B: J'ai douze ans.  → "I am 12."

sections.heading: Numbers 1–20
list: 1 un · 2 deux · 3 trois · 4 quatre · 5 cinq
6 six · 7 sept · 8 huit · 9 neuf · 10 dix
11 onze · 12 douze · 13 treize · 14 quatorze · 15 quinze
16 seize · 17 dix-sept · 18 dix-huit · 19 dix-neuf · 20 vingt

sections.heading: Pleased to meet you
sections.body: After someone tells you their name, the polite response is "Enchanté" (boy speaking) or "Enchantée" (girl speaking) — "Pleased to meet you". They sound exactly the same out loud; only the spelling differs.

teach_back.prompt: Tell a 7-year-old how to ask someone's age in French and what's weird about the answer.
checklist: Said you HAVE an age, not ARE one
teach_back.model_answer: Ask 'Quel âge as-tu?' — literally 'what age have you?' To answer, you say 'J'ai 12 ans' which means 'I have 12 years'. In French you HAVE an age, not ARE one — that's the classic trap.

tips: Use the LISTEN button on French questions to hear native pronunciation. Use SLOW for tricky bits.
"Je suis" = I AM. "J'ai" = I HAVE. Don't mix them up — age uses j'ai, not je suis.
Treize (13) and trente (30) sound similar but not the same. Treize ends with a soft 'z' sound; trente ends like 'tont'.
